quote: So we are unable to help you right now without anything built or, like, something to work off of with, like, a roof design or anything.
stewart_read: Corey DQs on the workshop-not-built basis without ever asking about the existing home at 376 Seville Road. The address was confirmed at 01:46 — Corey had it in hand. The strategic read would be that Corey recognized the workshop lead was premature and was correctly managing scope. The reactive read is that Corey accepted Andy's framing entirely and never introduced the existing home as a qualifying candidate. The reactive read holds: there is no evidence Corey considered the existing home, and the DQ language ('without anything built') is framed entirely around the future workshop.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey correctly identified that the workshop lead was not ready and issued a soft callback to preserve the relationship for when the build is complete.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Corey: 'When Andy said he wanted to put solar on the workshop roof, what did you think about the house he's already living in at that address?' The pivot line is simple — 'While we wait for the workshop to be ready, what does your current home look like for solar? We could potentially get you started there.' This is the move Corey needed to make before issuing the DQ.

quote: And we don't do ground mounts here just because they are super expensive and ultimately defeat the purpose of savings.
stewart_read: Corey delivers an editorial economics judgment — ground mounts are 'super expensive and ultimately defeat the purpose of savings' — that belongs to the closer, not the setter. The schema's scope_creep_signals explicitly flag 'rep quoting prices or savings ranges' and 'rep justifying company practices that customer questions.' Corey is not answering a customer question here; he is volunteering a cost-based rationale for a product limitation. This is setter-scope content delivered as a final verdict.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was trying to be transparent and save Andy time by explaining why ground mounts aren't offered. This is a reasonable customer-service instinct.
coaching_implication: The correct move is the universal escape hatch from the schema: 'Ground mounts are something our specialist can walk through with you — they'll have the full picture on what works for your property.' Corey's editorial closes the door on a conversation the closer should be having. In the 1-on-1, use this moment to reinforce the setter-scope boundary: Corey's job is to route, not to adjudicate product economics.

quote: I'm fairly certain they're now doing the selling of the power, but I'm not too certain on that.
stewart_read: Andy asks a specific, technical question about Rappahannock Electric Cooperative's net metering policy. Corey gives a hedged, uncertain answer rather than routing to the specialist. This is a double miss: (1) Corey is engaging in closer-tier content (utility net metering rules) outside setter scope, and (2) the answer is uncertain, which erodes credibility. The correct move is the universal escape hatch — 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer; they'll know Rappahannock's current policy' — which simultaneously holds scope and creates a reason to book.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was trying to be helpful and keep Andy engaged after the DQ. The hedged answer is better than silence.
coaching_implication: This is a textbook scope-creep moment that also happens to be a missed booking opportunity. In the 1-on-1, show Corey how the net metering question is a re-entry point: 'Andy, that's exactly what our specialist covers — they'll know Rappahannock's current setup. Let me get you on the calendar so you can get a real answer.' The question Andy is asking is the reason to book, not a reason to hedge.

quote: I would like us I would be interested in the system that has battery banks. Yep. And is that something you could you you could help help me with?
stewart_read: Andy volunteers an explicit interest in battery storage — a Tesla Powerwall product Ion carries. This is a direct enthusiasm signal: Andy is not just curious about solar, he wants a specific product configuration. Corey confirms capability ('Of course. Yes.') and ends the call. The confirmation is correct but the follow-through is absent. This is the second re-entry point Andy handed Corey after the DQ, and it was not used to pivot back to qualification or booking.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey had already issued the DQ and a soft callback; confirming battery capability was the right move to keep the door open without over-promising on a lead that isn't ready.
coaching_implication: Battery storage interest is a qualifying signal that raises lead value. In the 1-on-1, ask Corey: 'When Andy said he wanted battery banks, what did you think?' The pivot line: 'Battery storage is something our specialist designs into the system — that's actually a great reason to get you on the calendar, because the sizing depends on your home's load. What does your current home look like?' This converts Andy's enthusiasm signal into a qualification conversation about the existing home.

quote: I'd love to build a larger workshop this year, and I'm just gonna be revisiting so maybe putting solar on the roof a bit, or we have land for a, you know, pasture based solar panel system.
stewart_read: Andy's opening answer is rich with qualifying detail: a planned workshop build (new structure with a south-facing roofline), a ground-mount option on owned land, and a prior solar evaluation. This is not a vague 'I'm interested' — Andy has a project, a site, and a design preference already formed. Corey acknowledges the workshop framing but does not probe the depth of Andy's interest or the existing home. The verify.interest_in_solar item calls for 60+ seconds of depth here; Corey moves to address confirmation instead.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was correctly moving through the verify sequence and planned to return to interest depth later in the call.
coaching_implication: Andy's opening is the emotional anchor the entire call should have been built on. In the 1-on-1, replay this moment and ask Corey what he heard. The south-facing roofline detail, the land ownership, the prior evaluation — each is a thread that leads back to the existing home and to a booking. Top setters spend 60+ seconds here per the schema; Corey spent roughly 30 seconds before pivoting to address confirmation.

quote: Yeah. Most definitely. And, yeah, you are right. The government incentive did go away, the, tax credit. But, nonetheless, we do have our own program here in place to completely offset it. So no worries.
stewart_read: Andy raises the tax credit disappearing as a concern. Corey validates it and offers a vague 'our own program' redirect. This is the moment to anchor to Andy's bill pain — 'What are you paying on average right now?' — and flip the tax credit concern into a bill-savings conversation. Instead, Corey offers reassurance without grounding it in Andy's actual numbers. The bill is never captured in this call.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was moving efficiently through the intro and planned to capture the bill in the verify section.
coaching_implication: The schema's bill_anchoring pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' Corey acknowledged but did not ask or flip. In the 1-on-1, use this moment to introduce the flip: 'The tax credit going away is real — but what's also real is what you're paying Rappahannock every month. What does that look like for you?' This pivots Andy's concern about incentives into a bill-pain conversation that makes the appointment relevant regardless of federal programs.

quote: But, what I'll do here is I'll shoot you a text message with my personal number. So then once you have, like, a roof design built out and something like that, you can then give me a call directly, and we can help you out in getting an estimate started.
stewart_read: Corey issues a soft callback with no locked time and gives out his personal number as the follow-up mechanism. The schema explicitly flags this anti-pattern: 'rep gives out personal phone number for bill follow-up' is a detection signal for privacy/scale exposure. More critically, the callback has no time anchor — 'once you have a roof design built out' is an indefinite condition that makes this lead effectively cold. The schema's soft_outcome_distinction directive notes that callbacks with locked times vs 'we'll touch base' have a major delta on no-show rate.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was correctly managing a lead that isn't ready — issuing a personal number is a trust-building micro-trade that keeps the door open.
coaching_implication: Two issues for the 1-on-1: (1) personal number exposure — flag for the manager to review Ion's policy on this; the schema's automated bill capture section documents the privacy/scale risk. (2) No locked time — even a soft callback should have a specific follow-up date: 'Let me put a reminder on my end to reach back out in 30 days — does that work for you?' A locked callback is worth materially more than an open-ended 'call me when you're ready.'

quote: And then has the, like, metal barn been built? Is that what you're saying that was going to be built?
stewart_read: Corey's first verify question after address confirmation is about the workshop/barn — not about the existing home. This is the moment where the call's structural miss is set in motion. By leading with the workshop, Corey implicitly accepts Andy's framing that the workshop is the qualifying structure. The existing home at the confirmed address is never introduced. A single question here — 'And what about the home you're in now — is that the same property?' — would have opened the existing-home qualification path.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey was following Andy's lead and the workshop was the most salient detail from Andy's opening. Asking about the barn first was a rapport move.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, use this moment to show Corey the fork in the road: the barn question locks the call into a future-build frame, while a question about the existing home opens a present-tense qualification. The address was confirmed 9 seconds earlier — Corey had everything he needed to pivot. Suggested rephrase: 'Got it on the workshop — and what about the home you're in right now at that address? Is that a candidate too, or is the workshop the main focus?'
